I’m a songwriter and multi-instrumentalist who lives for that perfect resonance when the melody finally clicks into place. I spend my days chasing the right vibe, obsessing over tone, and finding the soul in the spaces between the notes.

I don’t really want to be anyone else because there’s something so honest about finding your own unique frequency; I’d rather spend my life perfecting my own sound and chasing that one perfect note that makes the room go still. If I were anyone else, I’d be losing the specific way my hands move across the strings and the way I hear the world in melodies, which is the only identity that actually feels real to me.

I’m a storyteller who uses strings and scales instead of words, finding the hidden rhythm in the silence between notes. Whether I'm chasing a perfect melody or feeling the resonance of a deep bass line, my life is basically just a constant search for the right frequency to make someone feel something.

Honestly, some days I just wish I were a masterpiece of pure, effortless resonance—a Stradivarius or a vintage Steinway that just exists to be felt. I’d love to be the sound itself, escaping the physical strain of the callouses and the heavy lifting of the gear, just to drift through the air as a perfect, haunting melody that lingers in a room long after the lights go down.

I’m originally from a small town where the acoustics were perfect for practicing in my garage until the neighbors started complaining, but honestly, I feel like I belong wherever the music is loud enough to make my chest rattle.

I’m here because the acoustics in this space just hit the sweet spot, and honestly, there’s nothing like the way the reverb carries the notes when you're finally in sync with the room. Music is about finding that perfect resonance, and right now, this is where the vibe feels most honest.

I’m the product of every late-night session where the notes finally clicked, every calloused fingertip, and that restless itch to turn a feeling into a frequency. I’m the way I am because I’ve spent my life chasing that perfect resonance, living for the moments where the notes hit just right and the room stops breathing. Honestly, it’s that visceral rush of hitting the right note or locking into a groove where everything just disappears except the sound. There's this incredible, almost spiritual connection that happens between the instrument and my hands, and when I finally share that feeling with an audience, it feels like we’re all breathing the same air for a few minutes. I do it because music is the only language that can say exactly what I'm feeling when words just fall short.

Honestly, it’s my bandmates—the only people who truly get the shorthand of a shared groove—and the crowd that shows up and lets us live in that space together for a few hours. But at the end of the day, I care about the music itself, because it's the only thing that feels honest when the lights go down and the noise finally stops.

I live for the resonance of a perfectly tuned cello and that fleeting, spiritual connection you feel with the band when the groove finally locks in. But honestly, I love the way a raw, honest melody can strip someone's defenses bare and turn a crowded room into a shared sanctuary of sound.

Honestly, I don’t have the headspace for hate—it's a total vibe killer. I’d rather just stay in the zone and let the music do the talking.

I’m constantly digging through a mix of everything from the old jazz greats and delta blues legends to the latest underground indie scene, but honestly, I spend most of my time analyzing the production on my favorite records to see how they layered the textures and captured the dynamics. I’m always hunting for that perfect reference track that makes me want to pick up my instrument and start writing something new.

Honestly, nothing matters more than finding that perfect resonance where the melody finally locks into the pocket of the rhythm. There’s nothing quite like that split second of perfect resonance when the room goes silent and the first note hangs in the air, vibrating right in your chest. Honestly, nothing kills my vibe faster than a room with terrible acoustics where the monitor mix is muddy and I can't hear my own timing. I just need that perfect, pocket-heavy groove and a room with enough natural reverb to let the notes breathe. If the vibe is right and the gear is dialed in, I’m ready to lay down some tracks—just give me a steady click and a little creative freedom to find the soul of the song.

Honestly, nothing hits quite like that moment when the room goes dead silent and the first note finally locks in with the rhythm. There’s nothing like that moment when the lights go down, the house falls silent, and I finally strike that first chord—it's that visceral connection between the notes and the crowd that makes every grueling rehearsal worth it. Honestly, nothing kills my vibe faster than dealing with "gear envy" from people who don't actually play, or having to spend three hours troubleshooting a faulty XLR cable right before the house lights go down. I need to find that perfect pocket of groove, lock in with the rest of the band, and make sure every note serves the emotion of the song rather than just showing off my technique. Honestly, my biggest struggle is definitely the "off-duty" life—I’m great at finding the perfect pocket in a groove or nailing a difficult transition, but I’m terrible at just sitting still and being productive in a way that doesn't involve a metronome or a blank staff. My brain is constantly looping melodies and analyzing harmonies, so when I'm trying to do something mundane like grocery shopping or folding laundry, my mind just drifts back to that one bridge I can’t quite get to sit right in my hands.

I’m best at finding that perfect pocket where the rhythm locks in and the melody just breathes, whether I'm shredding a complex solo or holding down a steady groove that makes the whole room move. Honestly, the only thing that keeps me up at night is the dreaded "silent room"—that heavy, suffocating pressure of a live crowd waiting for the first note to drop. Honestly, I’m not really "angry" in the way people think; I just get incredibly frustrated with gear that fails during a live set or a room's acoustics that completely swallow my tone. Honestly, the thing that keeps me up at night is stepping onto that stage and hitting a-flat note or losing my place in a complex bridge during a live set. Honestly, nothing gets under my skin more than having to play a gig where the monitors are completely blown out and the house mix is muddy as hell.
